results.possibleIdentity: Eric Grate (1896–1983) was a prominent Swedish sculptor, painter, and printmaker who remained active through the late 1960s and 1970s. He held a significant position as a professor at the Royal Academy of Art in Stockholm. In the early 1970s, he was associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his role as the chairman of a committee at the Moderna Museet in Stockholm. This committee was responsible for reviewing and accepting "The New York Collection for Stockholm," a donation of 30 works by leading New York artists organized by E.A.T. and its co-founder Billy Klüver. Grate's own modernist works, such as the sculpture 'Snäckfågel' (1967), are well-documented in the Swedish art scene of this period.

Sari Dienes (1898–1992) was an artist active in New York during the 1960s and 1970s whose identity is inextricably linked to "grates." She is famous for her large-scale monoprints and rubbings of urban textures, specifically Manhattan subway grates and manhole covers. Dienes was a close friend and significant influence on Robert Rauschenberg and John Cage, the core founders of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Although her surname is Dienes, she is frequently identified in historical art contexts by her work with grates, which she used to bridge the gap between found urban objects and abstract art.
